quizzes.title: Ground Lesson 1 — Orientation, Training Resources & Pre-flight

questions.question: When you set up ACH (Automated Clearing House) payment for your lessons, what is the processing fee charged compared to credit card payment?
options: ACH has no processing fee, unlike credit card which does
ACH and credit card have the same fee
ACH has a higher fee than credit card
The instructor hasn't decided which fee structure to use yet
questions.explanation: Desireé explained that ACH payments are free, while credit card payments incur a fee. She didn't specify the exact credit card fee amount, but confirmed that ACH is the fee-free option.

questions.question: In Flight Circle, when do you get charged for your flight time?
options: At a randomly scheduled time within the week
Immediately when the flight ends, after entering the Hobbs time
At the end of the month with a billing summary
You never get charged immediately; charges are disputed and resolved later
questions.explanation: Desireé stated: 'We land, clean up the plane, go in the hangar, I input the HOBBS intake time... We put it in Flight Circle, it spits out how long we flew and you get charged right there too.' Billing is instant and immediate.

questions.question: What happens to your lesson reservation if you need to cancel or switch days with less than full notice?
options: You forfeit the entire lesson cost with no refund
You don't get charged, but you have to wait 30 days before rebooking
You don't get charged if you cancel or switch days
You get a partial refund after administrative processing
questions.explanation: Desireé explained the cancellation policy: 'If we need to cancel anything... if we had to, like, say on Thursday, we, like, cancel or you switch days or something, you don't get any charge.' There is no charge for cancellations or schedule changes.

questions.question: According to Desireé, at what Sporty's practice test score should you schedule your official written test with the FAA?
options: When you consistently score in the 80s
When you consistently score in the 90s
When you reach a single score of 85 or higher
When you've completed all 50 practice questions at least once
questions.explanation: Desireé said: 'When you get to around like 90s, inconsistent 90s, we'll sign you up to go take your written test.' This is the target score before scheduling the official FAA test.

questions.question: What does the acronym 'ACS' stand for in the context of your Private Pilot training?
options: Aircraft Certification Standards
Airman Certification Standards
Aviation Crew Safety
Approved Checkride Subjects
questions.explanation: Desireé directly stated: 'ACS stands for Airman Certification Standards. Basically it's what your check ride is going to be on.' The ACS contains all the knowledge and skills you'll be tested on.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT one of the primary training resources Desireé assigned you to study from?
options: PHAK (Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge)
Airplane Flying Handbook
Bold Method YouTube videos
ForeFlight Premium subscription materials
questions.explanation: Desireé assigned PHAK, Airplane Flying Handbook, Bold Method, and Free Pilot Training as study resources. While she mentioned ForeFlight for flight planning and navigation, she did not assign ForeFlight Premium subscription materials as a training resource.

questions.question: Why does your flight school's checklist differ from generic 'Google version' checklists for aircraft?
options: The school checklists are simpler and faster to use in flight
They're tailored to the specific aircraft model and avionics you'll use
Generic checklists are not approved by the FAA
School checklists don't require you to verify as many items
questions.explanation: Desireé explained: 'Our school is just a little bit more detailed and we tailor it to every airplane because they're all a little different. Different avionics and different things you need to check.' Each checklist is customized to the specific aircraft and its equipment.

questions.question: When performing a preflight walkaround, what does checking for NOTAMs as part of your pre-flight action help you verify?
options: The condition of the aircraft's exterior paint and windscreen
Weather, ATC delays, runway lights, and operations at your home airport
The fuel type and quantity required for your flight
The correct calibration of the airspeed indicator
questions.explanation: Desireé described NOTAM checks as covering: 'weather, ACC delays, Runway links, flying out of our home airport.' NOTAMs (Notices to Airmen) alert you to operational hazards and restrictions relevant to your flight.

questions.question: According to Desireé's explanation, what is the primary effect of using flaps during landing?
options: Flaps reduce lift and drag equally, keeping the plane stable
Flaps increase lift but also increase drag, allowing slower landing speed
Flaps only increase drag without changing lift
Flaps are only used for emergency procedures, not normal landings
questions.explanation: Desireé stated: 'Flaps, that's kind of like our lift component, but with flaps, you get more lift but more drag. So you kind of use flaps when you're coming into land.' The combined effect allows you to descend at a slower airspeed during landing.

questions.question: What is the function of the trim system in the aircraft, and where is it physically located?
options: Trim adjusts engine power and is located on the throttle quadrant
Trim prevents spin recovery and is mounted on the fuselage
Trim alleviates control pressure so the pilot can fly with minimal force, and it's located on the right elevator
Trim is a backup flight control system located in the tail section
questions.explanation: Desireé explained: 'The trim wheel is inside the plane... the actual trim is located on your right elevator. It basically alleviates pressure so you're not constantly fighting when you fly.' Trim makes flying easier by reducing the control forces needed.

questions.question: Desireé recommended two specific online learning resources (besides PHAK and the Airplane Flying Handbook) to help break down principles of flight. One is Bold Method. What is the other?
options: Sporty's Flight Training (YouTube channel)
Free Pilot Training (YouTube channel)
FlightAware educational series
FAA's official online ground school
questions.explanation: Desireé said: 'So Bold Method... And then on YouTube there's a guy called Free pilot training that I love. He has really good stuff.' These provide abbreviated, visual explanations versus long textbook chapters.

questions.question: What is the purpose of dispatching your flight (or ground lesson) in Flight Circle, and what happens on the system when you do?
options: Dispatch submits a flight plan to ATC before every flight
Dispatch signals to the flight school that the lesson has started and turns the reservation a different color (green) in the system
Dispatch is only required for flights exceeding 2 hours in duration
Dispatch generates an automatic weather briefing for your route
questions.explanation: Desireé explained: 'So basically what we do before flight, your ground lesson, you hit dispatch... you'll see it turns a different color green and they just know that the ground lesson already started.' Dispatch is the school's confirmation that the lesson/flight is underway.

quizzes.title: Ground Lesson 2 — Principles of Flight & Aerodynamics

questions.question: What are the four forces acting on an aircraft during flight?
options: Lift, thrust, drag, and weight
Lift, pitch, roll, and yaw
Thrust, altitude, speed, and pressure
Lift, control forces, torque, and gyroscopic effects
questions.explanation: The four fundamental forces of flight are lift (upward force from airflow), thrust (forward force from engine), drag (resistance to motion), and weight (gravitational force). These form the foundation of aerodynamics principles.

questions.question: What are the two main types of drag that act on an aircraft?
options: Horizontal drag and vertical drag
Parasite drag and induced drag
Static drag and dynamic drag
Form drag and lift drag
questions.explanation: Parasite drag includes form drag, skin friction, and interference drag caused by the aircraft's shape. Induced drag is created as a byproduct of generating lift and is related to wingtip vortices.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what are the four left-turning tendencies mentioned?
options: Spiraling slipstream, gyroscopic precession, P-factor, and torque
Torque, weight shift, centrifugal force, and magnetic declination
Roll tendency, pitch tendency, yaw tendency, and bank tendency
Lift unbalance, thrust line offset, weight bias, and fuel shift
questions.explanation: The four left-turning tendencies are: spiraling slipstream (propeller wash over vertical stabilizer), gyroscopic precession (when the propeller-engine acts as a gyroscope), P-factor (propeller factor—blade entering different relative wind), and torque (engine rotation reaction).

questions.question: What physical phenomena at the aircraft wingtips create induced drag?
options: Ground effect interference patterns
Wingtip vortices formed by pressure differential between wing upper and lower surfaces
Shock waves from high-speed airflow
Friction between air molecules and wing surface material
questions.explanation: Wingtip vortices form because higher pressure air underneath the wing rolls around the wingtip to the lower-pressure area above. These vortices trail behind the aircraft and create induced drag, which is an unavoidable consequence of generating lift.

questions.question: How do Bernoulli's principle and Newton's third law both contribute to lift generation?
options: Bernoulli explains pressure difference; Newton's law explains reaction forces
They are competing theories; only one actually generates lift
Bernoulli applies only at high speeds; Newton applies only at low speeds
Both describe the same phenomenon but with different terminology
questions.explanation: Bernoulli's principle describes how faster-moving air over the curved upper wing surface creates lower pressure. Newton's third law explains the reaction force—air is deflected downward by the wing, and the wing experiences an equal and opposite upward force (lift).

questions.question: Why must you apply right rudder during takeoff in most single-engine aircraft?
options: To align the nose with the runway centerline
To counteract the left-turning tendency caused by spiraling slipstream, torque, and P-factor
To increase airspeed before rotation
To prevent the nose wheel from locking
questions.explanation: During takeoff, spiraling slipstream deflects air over the vertical stabilizer (pushing the nose left), engine torque rolls the aircraft left, and P-factor (high angle of attack) creates unequal blade lift. Right rudder input is needed to compensate and maintain directional control.

questions.question: In a banked turn, how does the horizontal component of lift affect the aircraft's behavior if pitch and throttle are not adjusted?
options: The aircraft maintains altitude and speed with no additional effect
The aircraft climbs slightly due to extra lift
The aircraft loses altitude and the pilot experiences increased G-forces
The aircraft accelerates horizontally without changing altitude
questions.explanation: When you bank the aircraft, total lift is now split between a vertical component (to support weight) and a horizontal component (to turn). If you don't adjust pitch or throttle, the vertical component decreases, causing descent, and you feel increased G-forces (load factor).

questions.question: What is the relationship between angle of attack, induced drag, and required thrust at lower airspeeds?
options: Higher angle of attack decreases induced drag and requires less thrust
Higher angle of attack increases induced drag and requires more thrust to maintain airspeed
Angle of attack and induced drag are unrelated
Induced drag decreases as airspeed decreases, regardless of angle of attack
questions.explanation: At lower airspeeds, you must pitch up to higher angles of attack to maintain lift. Higher angle of attack increases induced drag significantly. This increased drag requires more thrust to maintain the same airspeed.

questions.question: How do wingtip vortices behave when the aircraft is near the ground (ground effect)?
options: Vortices strengthen and increase induced drag
Vortices dissipate immediately and have no effect
Vortices hit the runway and are blocked from fully developing, reducing induced drag
Vortices change direction toward the fuselage
questions.explanation: In ground effect (near the runway), wingtip vortices cannot fully develop because they hit the runway surface. This disruption gives a small reduction in induced drag and provides a cushion effect—explaining why some aircraft seem to float near the ground during landing.

questions.question: You're climbing at a slower airspeed with a high angle of attack. Based on what you learned about induced drag, why might you need more throttle than in a shallow climb at higher airspeed?
options: The engine loses power in thin air
The high angle of attack generates large induced drag, requiring more thrust to overcome it
Slow climbing damages engine efficiency
The propeller becomes inefficient at steep pitch angles
questions.explanation: Higher angles of attack (needed at low airspeeds to generate lift) create large induced drag. This drag multiplies with the angle of attack, requiring significantly more thrust to maintain a constant slow airspeed and climb rate. This principle is critical for short-field operations.

questions.question: You're entering a steep turn (30° bank) without adjusting pitch or throttle. Predict what happens and why.
options: The aircraft maintains altitude because the horizontal component of lift helps
The aircraft climbs because total lift increases with bank angle
The aircraft descends because the vertical lift component decreases, and load factor increases
The aircraft flies straight due to gyroscopic stability
questions.explanation: When you bank without adjusting pitch/throttle, the total lift vector tilts. The vertical component (which supports weight) decreases while a horizontal component develops (needed for turning). Insufficient vertical lift causes descent. The load factor (feeling of G's) increases because total lift is shared between vertical and horizontal components.

questions.question: What study resources did your instructor recommend for deeply understanding principles of flight?
options: Only the FAA's official online ground school
PHAK, Airplane Flying Handbook, Bold Method, and Free Pilot Training (YouTube)
Sporty's practice tests exclusively
The ACS and checkride prep guides
questions.explanation: Your instructor emphasized using multiple resources: PHAK (Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge) Chapters 4–5, the Airplane Flying Handbook, Bold Method videos, and Free Pilot Training (YouTube) to see these concepts broken down visually. Different sources help tie concepts together.

quizzes.title: Ground Lesson 3 — ATIS, ATC Comms, Taxi Ops & FAR 61.113

questions.question: What is the KSNA-mandatory wake-turbulence procedure on Runway 20L departure?
options: A right turn to heading 200° at 1000 ft AGL
A 15° LEFT turn to heading 181° (offset turn)
A 30° right turn at the upwind threshold
A straight-out climb until 2000 ft, then turn
questions.explanation: KSNA requires a mandatory 15° LEFT turn to heading 181° at the end of Runway 20L departure to clear wake turbulence from the parallel runway. Desireé noted this is binding in the ATIS/clearance and there's been a near-miss tied to non-compliance — checkride examiners love asking the WHY.

questions.question: Under FAR 61.113(c), if three people fly a $300 trip together, how must the cost be split?
options: The pilot pays nothing — passengers cover the full cost as compensation
Each person pays exactly $100 (pro-rata equal share); pilot CANNOT have passengers subsidize their share
The pilot pays a token $1, passengers split the rest
Whatever they agree to in writing before the flight
questions.explanation: FAR 61.113(c) requires PRO-RATA cost sharing — pilot pays AT LEAST an equal share. 3 people, $300 → each pays $100. Only fuel, oil, airport fees, and rental count. Cannot include pilot time, maintenance, or insurance. Cannot have passengers subsidize the pilot's share.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'Monitor [frequency]' and 'Contact [frequency]' from ATC?
options: They mean the same thing — switch frequencies and check in
Monitor = switch and listen only, do NOT transmit. Contact = switch AND transmit a position report
Monitor is for VFR only; Contact is for IFR only
Monitor = stay on current freq and listen; Contact = switch freq
questions.explanation: MONITOR means switch the frequency but do NOT transmit (e.g., 'Roger, monitoring 119.9'). CONTACT means switch the frequency AND transmit a position report (e.g., 'Tower, Skyhawk 51840…'). ATC will tell you which — listen carefully.

questions.question: What is the correct phraseology for saying the number 9 in ATC communications, and why?
options: 'Nine' — same as everyday English
'Niner' — to distinguish from 'five' on a noisy frequency
'Nina' — phonetic alphabet convention
'Nin' — abbreviated form for radio efficiency
questions.explanation: Always say 'NINER' for 9 (not 'nine') to distinguish from 'five' on a noisy frequency. Note: do NOT say 'fife' for 5 or 'tree' for 3 — just 'five' and 'three.' Niner is the only number that gets the special pronunciation.

questions.question: When tower says 'say altitude' during your departure climb, what altitude should you state?
options: Your assigned altitude (e.g., 'climbing to 2400')
Your CURRENT actual altitude (e.g., '1400 climbing to 2400')
The traffic pattern altitude (856 ft for KSNA)
The MSL altitude shown on your altimeter, no other context
questions.explanation: 'Say altitude' wants your CURRENT actual altitude, not the assigned target. Readback example: 'Skyhawk 51840, 1400 climbing to 2400.' This shows tower you're aware of your aircraft's current performance. Order of elements is flexible as long as all info is there.

questions.question: What does the 'DIVE AWAY' mnemonic mean for crosswind taxi?
options: Dive away from oncoming traffic by rolling to the right
Push yoke FORWARD and turn AWAY from the wind direction (ailerons INTO the wind)
Dive (push down) the throttle AWAY from the wind to reduce groundspeed
Avoid the wind by taxiing on a different taxiway
questions.explanation: DIVE AWAY: push the yoke FORWARD and turn the ailerons AWAY from the direction the wind is coming from. This effectively rolls the upwind wing DOWN (ailerons INTO the wind). Example: wind from the left → push forward + turn yoke right. Desireé called this the golden nugget of the lesson.

questions.question: Under FAR 61.113(b) (incidental to business), can a private pilot be reimbursed for fuel when flying for business purposes?
options: Yes, in all business contexts — no restrictions
Yes, ONLY if the flying is INCIDENTAL to the business and NOT the primary business function
No — private pilots can never accept any compensation related to business flights
Only if the pilot has more than 500 hours total time
questions.explanation: FAR 61.113(b) allows reimbursement for fuel/oil if flying is INCIDENTAL to the business, not the primary business function. A doctor flying to pick up supplies = OK. A charter pilot or cargo run = NOT OK. This is a gray area for SkyAccess operator-outreach flights — Desireé flagged it as needing written guidance from Sergio/chief instructor.

questions.question: What altitude should you start your turn to assigned heading after a KSNA departure?
options: 1000 ft AGL — universal pattern altitude
500 ft AGL practical (technically 300 ft below TPA = 556 ft, rounds to 600)
Immediately after wheels-up
Pattern altitude of 856 ft AGL
questions.explanation: 500 ft AGL is the practical turn-to-heading altitude. Per FAR 91.181 the technical rule is 300 ft below traffic pattern altitude — at KSNA TPA = 856 ft, so 556 ft (rounds to 600). On hot/heavy days, climb an extra 100 ft before turning.

questions.question: What does 'Wilco' mean and when is it appropriate to use?
options: 'Will compromise' — used when negotiating with ATC
'Will comply + acknowledged' — useful to shorten long readbacks
'With clearance' — confirms you have a clearance
'Wait, listen, copy, observe' — used when you missed a transmission
questions.explanation: WILCO = 'Will Comply + acknowledged.' It combines Roger (received) with a commitment to do what was asked. Useful for shortening long readbacks, e.g., 'Wilco, 120.7, Skyhawk 840.' Note: ROGER alone just means 'received' (NOT 'yes').

questions.question: How does a windsock indicate wind speed?
options: By its color — red = strong winds, white = calm
Fully inflated ≈ 15 kt; each stripe ≈ 3 kt; opening points DOWNWIND
It rotates faster as wind increases — count rotations per minute
By the angle to vertical — 45° = 10 kt, 90° = 20 kt
questions.explanation: A windsock fully inflated ≈ 15 knots. Each stripe ≈ 3 knots additional. The OPENING (large end) points downwind; the inflation points INTO the wind. Used at non-towered fields and as a backup at towered fields for runway selection and taxi wind correction.

questions.question: What is the correct initial call to KSNA Clearance Delivery (118.0) for a VFR flight following request?
options: 'Clearance, ready to taxi'
'Clearance, Skyhawk 51840 is a Cessna 172 Uniform at Dove Street requesting VFR flight following to [destination] with information [letter]'
'Tower, Skyhawk 51840, IFR clearance please'
'Ground, Skyhawk 51840 ready to go'
questions.explanation: Initial Clearance Delivery call must include: aircraft type + equipment suffix (172 Uniform = basic equipment, no ILS), parking location (Dove Street), destination, flight following request, and the ATIS information letter you have. Desireé recommends writing the script on the ForeFlight scratch pad and reading it verbatim until it's automatic — KSNA controllers don't have time for hesitation.

questions.question: Under FAR 61.113(f), what is the minimum PIC time required to demonstrate an aircraft to a prospective buyer?
options: 100 hours PIC
200 hours PIC
500 hours PIC
No minimum — any PPL holder can do salesman demos
questions.explanation: FAR 61.113(f) requires ≥200 hours of logged PIC time before a private pilot can demonstrate an aircraft in flight to a prospective buyer. The other 61.113 exceptions to memorize: (b) incidental to business, (c) pro-rata cost sharing, (d) charity per 91.146, (e) SAR ops (agency-initiated only), (g) glider/ultralight tow with 61.69 endorsement, (h) light-sport production test (≥100 hrs PIC in category).

quizzes.title: Ground Lesson 4 — METARs, Hazardous Attitudes & Runway Incursions

questions.question: When a METAR reports the wind group as 18011G18KT, how should you read it out loud on a checkride?
options: Winds one hundred eighty at eleven to eighteen knots
Winds one eight zero at 11, gusting 18 knots
Winds one eight zero at 11 to 18
Winds 180 at 11 with 18 knot maximum
questions.explanation: Direction is spoken digit-by-digit ('one eight zero'), wind is 'AT' (not 'at to'), and the gust value is the upper number — never read it as 'at 11 to 18'. Desireé corrected this exact mistake during the lesson.

questions.question: What does P6SM mean in a METAR?
options: Pressure 6 standard millibars
Plus 6 statute miles — visibility greater than 6 SM (exact value not reported)
Precipitation 6 statute miles
Prevailing 6 statute miles
questions.explanation: P6SM = Plus 6 Statute Miles. METARs encode visibility above 6 SM as P6SM rather than reporting an exact figure. Desireé: 'They'll never say 17 statute miles of visibility — past 6 you'll see P6SM.'

questions.question: On a METAR, what is the difference between AO1 and AO2?
options: AO1 is automated; AO2 is augmented (a human at the station)
AO1 is automated WITHOUT precipitation discrimination; AO2 is automated WITH precipitation discrimination
AO1 is a primary station; AO2 is a backup station
AO1 reports cloud heights; AO2 reports cloud type
questions.explanation: Both are automated. AO2 stations can tell rain from snow (precipitation discrimination); AO1 stations cannot. Almost every modern automated station is AO2.

questions.question: How is a runway number derived from the magnetic bearing of the runway?
options: Magnetic bearing × 10
Magnetic bearing rounded to the nearest 10°, with the last digit dropped
Magnetic bearing minus 360
Magnetic bearing converted to true bearing
questions.explanation: Round the magnetic bearing to the nearest 10° and drop the last digit. Heading 198° → 'Runway 20.' Lowest possible runway number is 01; highest is 36.

questions.question: What is the reciprocal of Runway 09?
options: Runway 18
Runway 27
Runway 09 reciprocal
Runway 36
questions.explanation: Reciprocal math: ±20 (the drop-the-zero version of ±180°). 09 + 18 = 27. Same physical runway, opposite direction.

questions.question: On the airport diagram, what do the L/C/R suffixes after a runway number indicate, and from whose perspective?
options: Left/Center/Right designation for parallel runways, assigned from the approach side
Light/Center/Right based on lighting equipment installed on the runway
Left/Center/Right always assigned looking north on the airport diagram
Long/Cross/Reciprocal — runway use designation
questions.explanation: L/C/R designate parallel runways and are assigned from the approach side. KSNA has 20L and 20R; if winds shift, the same physical strips become 02L and 02R from the new approach direction.

questions.question: Name the five hazardous attitudes the FAA identifies that degrade pilot decision-making.
options: Anti-authority, Impulsivity, Invulnerability, Macho, Resignation
Anti-authority, Anxiety, Inattention, Macho, Recklessness
Aggression, Impulsivity, Indecision, Macho, Resignation
Anti-authority, Impulsivity, Invulnerability, Bravado, Surrender
questions.explanation: The five FAA-identified hazardous attitudes are Anti-authority, Impulsivity, Invulnerability, Macho, and Resignation. Each has a specific antidote — the written test pairs them.

questions.question: What is the antidote to the hazardous attitude IMPULSIVITY ('do it now')?
options: Follow the rules. They are usually right.
It could happen to me.
Not so fast. Think first.
Taking chances is foolish.
questions.explanation: Impulsivity ('do it now') is countered by 'Not so fast. Think first.' Desireé failed her first CFI checkride to impulsivity — turning downwind early because she debated the clearance in her head instead of using the G1000 playback button.

questions.question: What is the antidote to the hazardous attitude RESIGNATION ('what's the use')?
options: Follow the rules. They are usually right.
It could happen to me.
Not so fast. Think first.
I'm not helpless. I can make a difference.
questions.explanation: Resignation ('what's the use') is countered by 'I'm not helpless. I can make a difference.' Example: alternator fails with 30 minutes of battery left — a resignation pilot gives up; the antidote pilot works the problem.

questions.question: What does CFIT stand for, and what is the typical accident scenario?
options: Critical Flight Into Turbulence — pilot encounters severe turbulence and loses control
Controlled Flight Into Terrain — pilot flies a fully airworthy airplane into terrain due to a decision-making failure (often VFR pilot pushing into IMC)
Catastrophic Failure In Transit — engine failure during cruise
Crew Failure In Training — multi-pilot coordination breakdown
questions.explanation: CFIT = Controlled Flight Into Terrain. The plane is fully airworthy; the failure is in pilot decision-making. Classic setup: VFR-only pilot pushes into deteriorating weather, gets into IMC, becomes spatially disoriented, descends into a mountain (KSNA local hazard: Santiago Peak).

questions.question: If you accidentally enter IMC as a VFR-only pilot, what is the correct radio call?
options: Switch to Guard 121.5 silently and try to fly out yourself
'Mayday, mayday, mayday — Skyhawk 51840 — IMC, request vectors out of cloud, no longer VFR.'
Wait until conditions clear; only declare if it doesn't improve in 10 minutes
Call the nearest tower for landing clearance
questions.explanation: Declare an emergency immediately with three 'mayday' calls. Don't try to hide it or fly out yourself — ATC will vector you out. Desireé: 'It's an emergency. You declare an emergency.'

questions.question: What is the FAA definition of a runway incursion?
options: Any aircraft landing on the wrong runway during night operations
Any occurrence at an aerodrome involving the incorrect presence of an aircraft, vehicle, or person on the protected area of a surface designated for the landing and takeoff of aircraft
Any time two aircraft come within 500 feet of each other on the airport surface
Any deviation from the assigned taxi route by more than one taxiway
questions.explanation: This is the FAA's verbatim definition. Memorize it word-for-word — DPEs probe runway incursions heavily on CFI checkrides and they show up at towered fields too, not just non-towered.

questions.question: List the four runway-incursion severity categories from worst (A) to best (D).
options: A: minor confusion · B: missed taxiway · C: wrong altitude · D: late report
A: barely-avoided collision · B: significant potential for collision · C: ample time and distance to avoid collision · D: little to no risk of collision
A: aircraft involved · B: vehicle involved · C: pedestrian involved · D: animal involved
A: at night · B: in low visibility · C: during high winds · D: during normal ops
questions.explanation: Categories A through D rank severity. A is barely-avoided collision (worst); D is little/no risk (mildest). The FAA tracks all four — not just the dramatic ones.

questions.question: Why is the 1977 Tenerife disaster (KLM 4805 + Pan Am 1736) cited in runway-incursion training?
options: It was the first runway incursion ever recorded

It is the worst aviation disaster in history (~583 fatalities) and the textbook example of why pilots must read back every clearance — KLM started take-off without clearance while Pan Am was still back-taxiing on the same runway in low visibility
It established the modern severity-categorization system
It led to the creation of ATIS
questions.explanation: Tenerife is the canonical 'why we read back clearances' case. Low-vis day, runway being used as a taxiway. KLM rolled without clearance while Pan Am was still on the runway. ~583 fatalities — the worst aviation disaster on record.

questions.question: Define 'movement area' and 'non-movement area' on an airport surface.
options: Movement = where vehicles can drive; non-movement = pedestrian-only zones
Movement = all taxiways and runways under the jurisdiction of the control tower (clearance required to enter); non-movement = ramps and parking, not controlled by ATC, no clearance needed
Movement = active runways only; non-movement = inactive runways and all taxiways
Movement = areas with painted markings; non-movement = unpaved surfaces
questions.explanation: Movement areas are under tower jurisdiction — clearance required to enter or cross. Non-movement areas (ramps, parking) are not controlled — you can move/taxi/push without a call.

questions.question: A solid yellow line and a dashed yellow line meet at the boundary between a ramp and a taxiway. Which side is which?
options: Solid = movement side (clearance required to cross); dashed = non-movement
Solid = non-movement side (you stop here); dashed = movement side (clearance required to cross)
Solid = active during day; dashed = active during night
Solid = aircraft only; dashed = vehicles only
questions.explanation: Solid yellow is the boundary on the non-movement side — you stop. Dashed yellow is on the movement side — clearance is required to cross. Mnemonic: 'dash = movement, solid = stopped.'

quizzes.title: Ground Lesson 5 — Stalls, Spins, PARE & KSNA Base-to-Final

questions.question: What is the verbatim cause of a stall that the DPE will ask you to recite?
options: Reaching a specific minimum airspeed below which the wing cannot produce lift
Loss of engine power that prevents the wing from generating lift
Exceeding the critical angle of attack — the angle between the chord line and the relative wind
An imbalance between thrust and drag during slow flight
questions.explanation: A stall is the result of exceeding the critical angle of attack — not exceeding a specific airspeed. You can stall at any airspeed if you exceed the critical AoA. This is a direct verbatim question on the oral.

questions.question: List the four phases of a stall in order.
options: Approach, entry, full stall, recovery
Entry, incipient, developed, recovery
Initiation, propagation, autorotation, escape
Pre-stall, stall, spin, return
questions.explanation: Entry → Incipient → Developed → Recovery. Incipient is just starting (wing dips, nose turns). Developed is the full stall (or spin if uncoordinated). Easiest recovery is in the incipient phase.

questions.question: Per ACS, what is the lowest altitude at which an ASEL applicant may complete a stall recovery?
options: 1,000 ft AGL
1,500 ft AGL
3,000 ft AGL
500 ft AGL
questions.explanation: ASEL: NO LOWER than 1,500 ft AGL. AMEL is 3,000 ft AGL but doesn't apply to a single-engine PPL. Recovering below 1,500 ft AGL on a checkride busts the ride. Aryan flies the maneuver at 3,500 AGL for a 2,000 ft buffer.

questions.question: The ACS skills standard for power-on/power-off stalls allows what heading and bank tolerances?
options: Heading ±5°, bank max 30°
Heading ±10°, bank max 20° (±10°)
Heading ±15°, bank max 45°
No tolerance — must be exact
questions.explanation: Heading ±10° if straight flight; max bank 20° with ±10° tolerance if turning flight. Exceeding bank invites a spin entry — that's WHY the bank limit exists.

questions.question: In a power-on stall, the ACS specifies the power setting must be no less than what value?
options: 50% power
65% power
75% power
Full throttle
questions.explanation: ACS power-on stalls: as assigned by the evaluator, no less than 65% power. NEVER full throttle — at full power the plane refuses to stall and you'd have to over-pitch into a dangerous attitude. For N51840, just past half power is the sweet spot.

questions.question: Recite the call-outs for a power-OFF stall in order.
options: Mushy controls → stall horn → stall
Stall horn → buffeting → mushy controls → stall
Mushy controls → stall horn → buffeting → stall
Buffeting → stall horn → stall → recovery
questions.explanation: Power-off has FOUR call-outs: mushy controls → stall horn → buffeting (plane shakes) → stall. Power-on has only THREE (no buffeting because the high-pitch attitude with power doesn't develop the same shake). Don't confuse them.

questions.question: TWO conditions must BOTH be true for an aircraft to spin. What are they?
options: High bank angle and low airspeed
Stalled wing and uncoordinated flight (yawing)
Engine failure and loss of altitude
Aft CG and high pitch attitude
questions.explanation: (1) STALLED + (2) UNCOORDINATED. Either alone won't spin you. That is exactly why the ★ 'stay coordinated' rule appears in both stall procedures — it prevents the spin even if you stall.

questions.question: Spell out PARE — the spin recovery acronym — letter by letter.
options: Pitch · Aileron · Recover · Engine
Power idle · Ailerons neutral · Rudder OPPOSITE · Elevator forward
Pull · Add power · Roll level · Exit
Pitch up · Add rudder · Reduce power · Engage trim
questions.explanation: P = Power idle (keeps spin from flattening). A = Ailerons neutral (aileron adds INTO the spin). R = Rudder OPPOSITE the spin (counters yaw drag). E = Elevator forward (breaks AoA, un-stalls).

questions.question: You are spinning to the LEFT. Which rudder do you push for recovery, and why?
options: Left rudder — it follows the spin direction
Right rudder — opposite the spin direction
Whichever rudder is most comfortable
Rudder is irrelevant; pitch alone recovers the spin
questions.explanation: RIGHT rudder — opposite the spin. Spin LEFT = RIGHT rudder. Spin RIGHT = LEFT rudder. The R in PARE = 'Rudder OPPOSITE.' In IMC, trust the turn coordinator over your inner ear (somatogravic illusion).

questions.question: Which spin type is harder to recover from, and what CG condition causes it?
options: Steep spin — caused by forward CG
Flat spin — caused by aft CG
Steep spin — caused by aft CG
Flat spin — caused by forward CG
questions.explanation: FLAT spin = horizontal nose attitude · AFT CG · very hard to recover. The horizontal stabilizer is an upside-down airfoil that produces downforce; with insufficient airflow, it can't push the nose down to break the stall. STEEP spin = nose-down · forward CG · easier to recover. Loading aft CG raises flat-spin risk.

questions.question: Why do pilots stall on the base-to-final turn at KSNA?
options: Engine power loss caused by descending below the green arc
Overshooting final, leading with rudder, becoming uncoordinated, and unintentionally pitching up — stall + uncoordinated at ~600 ft AGL = spin with no recovery time
Excessive bank angle alone, without any rudder input
Crosswind shear unique to KSNA's location
questions.explanation: The killer pattern: pilot overshoots final, tries to compensate with RUDDER (yaw input), doesn't add aileron with it → uncoordinated → unintentionally pitches up to hold altitude → stalled + uncoordinated = SPIN at 550–650 ft AGL with essentially no recovery time. Defense: turn EARLY rather than late. Overshoot? Go around.

questions.question: In N51840 (no digital lean assist), how do you lean the mixture above 3,000 MSL?
options: Pull the mixture all the way out, then push half-way back in
Twist the mixture out, watch the RPMs rise to peak then fall, then turn the mixture IN three times
Set the mixture to 50% by feel and adjust based on engine sound
Adjust the mixture only when the engine begins to roughen
questions.explanation: Twist the mixture OUT → watch RPMs rise to peak then fall → turn the mixture IN three full turns. That's your best fuel/air ratio for the current altitude. If the engine gets rough or gauges quiver, you've over-leaned — twist back IN slightly. G1000 has a digital lean-assist that targets 1,450 EGT; N51840 uses the visual RPM method.

quizzes.title: Ground Lesson 6 — Airworthiness, Documents, Inspections & FAR 91.205

questions.question: Which is the verbatim 3-prong definition of airworthiness?
options: Plane has all maintenance signed off and is owned by a US citizen
Plane meets approved type design, is in condition for safe flight, and all maintenance/required inspections/directives are done
Plane has a current annual and passes the preflight inspection
Plane has all SPARROW documents in the cabin and a valid registration
questions.explanation: All three prongs must hold. Maintenance alone is not enough — the plane must also meet the approved type design AND be in condition for safe flight. This is the verbatim DPE answer.

questions.question: Who has the responsibility to maintain the aircraft in airworthy condition, and who has the final authority to determine if it's safe to fly today?
options: PIC maintains; A&P decides safety
Owner maintains; FAA decides safety
Owner maintains; PIC has final authority on safety that day
A&P-IA maintains; owner has final authority
questions.explanation: Two layers: Owner = maintenance responsibility. PIC = final authority on whether to fly today. Example: bald tires + wet runway = airworthy but PIC no-gos.

questions.question: Spell out SPARROW.
options: Supplements, Placards, Airworthiness, Registration, Radio license, Operating handbook, Weight & balance
Standards, Placards, Annual, Registration, Radio, Owner manual, Weight
Supplements, POH, Airworthiness, Registration, Radio, Owner license, W&B sheet
Standards, Permit, Airworthiness, Registration, Radio, Operating manual, Weight
questions.explanation: Supplements · Placards · Airworthiness · Registration · Radio operator license (intl only) · Operating handbook (POH) · Weight & balance sheet.

questions.question: How long is the airworthiness certificate valid?
options: 12 calendar months
24 calendar months
7 years
Indefinitely — for the lifetime of the plane, as long as inspections remain current
questions.explanation: Airworthiness is valid INDEFINITELY for the life of the aircraft, conditional on required inspections being current. Common wrong answers: 12 or 24 months (those are inspection intervals).

questions.question: How long is the aircraft registration valid?
options: 2 years
3 years
5 years
7 years
questions.explanation: 7 years. It used to be 2 years until COVID-era changes bumped it to 7. Some students still guess 2 from older study materials.

questions.question: Who can sign off an annual inspection, and who can sign off a 100-hour?
options: Any A&P can do both
A&P-IA only does annuals; A&P alone can do 100-hour. Annual overrides 100-hour; 100-hour never substitutes for annual
A&P does annual; A&P-IA does 100-hour
Only the manufacturer can sign either
questions.explanation: A&P = Airframe & Powerplant (can do 100-hour). A&P-IA = A&P with Inspection Authorization (can do annual). One credential, not two. Annual overrides 100-hour; a 100-hour can NEVER replace an annual.

questions.question: AAV1ATE/88 intervals — what's the ELT interval?
options: 24 calendar months
12 calendar months OR half battery life OR 1 hour cumulative use
30 days
100 hours
questions.explanation: ELT has THREE triggers — whichever comes first: 12 calendar months · half battery life · 1 hour cumulative use. The other inspection intervals: Annual 12 mo, 100-hr 100 tach hrs (for hire), Altimeter/Transponder 24 mo, VOR 30 days (IFR only).

questions.question: What's the difference between an AD and an SB?
options: AD is for engines, SB is for airframe
AD is FAA-issued and mandatory; SB is manufacturer-issued and recommendation only (unless folded into an AD)
AD applies to Part 135, SB applies to Part 91
They're the same thing — manufacturers issue both
questions.explanation: AD (Airworthiness Directive) = FAA · MANDATORY compliance. SB (Service Bulletin) = manufacturer (Textron/Cessna) · RECOMMENDATION only unless the FAA later wraps it into an AD. SB = 'sister of an AD.'

questions.question: DPE asks: 'The landing-gear tires look flat — could you replace one?' What's the scripted correct answer?
options: No — only an A&P can touch the tires
Yes — pilots can do anything under preventative maintenance
'Per FAR Part 43 Appendix A under preventative maintenance, I can. I don't know how to do it, so I never would do it without help. But legally I can.'
I'd call the FSDO and ask
questions.explanation: FAR Part 43 Appendix A Paragraph C authorizes pilots to perform listed preventative maintenance items including tire replacement. The DPE wants the verbatim 'legally I can, but I'd ask for help' framing — confidence in the regulation, humility about the wrenches.

questions.question: Recite A-TOMATO-FLAMES (VFR Day, FAR 91.205(b)).
options: Airspeed, Tach, Oil pressure, Manifold press, Altimeter, Temp (liquid), Oil temp (air), Fuel, Landing gear position (retractable), Anti-collision, Mag compass, ELT, Safety belts
Altimeter, Tach, Oil press, Magneto, Anti-collision, Temp, Oil filter, Fuses, Lights, Anti-ice, Mag compass, Engine, Seats
ADs, Tach, Owner manual, Maintenance log, Altimeter, TCDS, Owner approval, Fuel, Lights, AAV1ATE, MEL, ELT, Safety belts
All of the above
questions.explanation: Airspeed · Tachometer · Oil pressure · Manifold press · Altimeter · Temperature (liquid-cooled engines) · Oil temp (air-cooled engines) · Fuel gauges · Landing gear position (retractable) · Anti-collision lights (post 3/11/1996) · Magnetic compass · ELT · Safety belts.

questions.question: What does FLAPS add for VFR Night (FAR 91.205(c))?
options: Fuel pump, Lights, Anti-ice, Pitot heat, Source of power
Fuses (or Circuit breakers), Landing light (if for hire), Anti-collision, Position lights, Source of electrical power
Flap motor, Landing gear, Altimeter, POH, Static source
Fire extinguisher, Lights, Altimeter, POH, Seat belts
questions.explanation: Fuses (or Circuit breakers — N51840 has CBs, hence the '(C)') · Landing light (only if for hire) · Anti-collision lights · Position lights · Source of electrical power. These are ADDITIONS to A-TOMATO-FLAMES for night ops.

questions.question: Inoperative equipment: what's the correct order of the 5 things to check?
options: FAR 91.205 → MEL → KOEL → CEL → TCDS
MEL → TCDS → KOEL → CEL → FAR 91.205 (+ ADs/STCs/ICAs)
CEL → KOEL → MEL → TCDS → FAR 91.205
ADs → MEL → POH → CEL → 91.205
questions.explanation: Order matters. MEL (Part 121/135 + type-rated; Part 91 doesn't require) → TCDS ('birth certificate') → KOEL (POH Sec 2) → CEL (POH Sec 6 with R/S/O/A suffixes) → FAR 91.205 plus ADs/STCs/ICAs. Then DO: disable, INOP-placard, PEDS log. DECIDE: go/no-go.

quizzes.title: Ground Lesson 6 (continued) — KSNA Airspace & FAR 91.155 VFR Weather Minimums

questions.question: Basic VFR weather minimums for Class C (and B/D/E below 10,000)?
options: 1 SM, clear of clouds
3 SM · 1,000 above · 500 below · 2,000 horizontal
5 SM · 1,000 above · 1,000 below · 1 SM horizontal
3 SM · 500 below · 1,000 above · 2,000 horizontal
questions.explanation: 3 SM · 1,000 above · 500 below · 2,000 horizontal. Same day and night. Mnemonic cadence: '3-1-5-2.' Also applies to Class B, D, and E below 10,000 MSL.

questions.question: Basic VFR weather mins for Class G DAY, at or below 1,200 ft AGL?
options: 1 SM · 1,000 above · 500 below · 2,000 horizontal
3 SM · clear of clouds
1 SM · clear of clouds (no feet entry)
questions.explanation: 1 SM visibility · CLEAR OF CLOUDS. The horizontal slot has no feet entry — judgment-based distance. Practical: not IN a cloud, but you can be right above or below one.

questions.question: Basic VFR weather mins for Class G NIGHT (below 10,000)?
options: 1 SM · clear of clouds
1 SM · 1,000 above · 500 below · 2,000 horizontal
3 SM · 500 below · 1,000 above · 2,000 horizontal
5 SM · 1,000 above · 1,000 below · 1 SM horizontal
questions.explanation: 3 SM · 500 below · 1,000 above · 2,000 horizontal. Class G night is more restrictive than day — that's why the burger-trapped-at-night scenario works as a DPE question.

questions.question: Basic VFR weather mins ABOVE 10,000 MSL (Echo and Golf)?
options: 5 SM · 1,000 above · 1,000 below · 1 STATUTE MILE horizontal
5 SM · 1,000 above · 500 below · 2,000 horizontal
3 SM · 1,000 above · 1,000 below · 2,000 horizontal
1 SM · clear of clouds
questions.explanation: 5 SM · 1,000 above · 1,000 below · 1 STATUTE MILE horizontal — the only place the horizontal slot flips from FEET to MILES. Mnemonic cadence: '5-1-1-1.'

questions.question: What does 'clear of clouds' literally mean in 91.155?
options: At least 2,000 ft from any visible cloud
Above the cloud layer with 500 ft buffer
Not IN a cloud — but you can be right above or right below; no fixed feet entry, judgment-based distance
Visibility unobstructed to 5 SM
questions.explanation: Literally not IN a cloud. No specific feet — judgment-based. Just don't get yourself into IMC. Mental yardstick: 1 statute mile ≈ 5,280 ft from your wing if you need a number.

questions.question: How is 1 statute mile measured?
options: Approximately 5,280 ft
Approximately 6,076 ft (same as nautical mile)
Exactly 1,000 m
Approximately 3,280 ft
questions.explanation: 1 statute mile ≈ 5,280 ft. (1 nautical mile ≈ 6,076 ft — weather is reported in STATUTE miles, distance in NAUTICAL miles.) Useful mental yardstick for 'clear of clouds' judgment.

questions.question: KSNA airspace stack — Class Charlie altitudes?
options: Surface to 4,000 ft
3,500 to 4,400 ft (sectional altitudes written in hundreds)
Surface to 10,000 ft
questions.explanation: Class Charlie at KSNA = 3,500 to 4,400 ft. Sectional altitudes are written in HUNDREDS — '35/44' = 3,500/4,400. Must be talking to John Wayne Tower inside; handoff to SoCal exits the shelf.

questions.question: At KSNA, where does Class E start in the magenta-shaded areas?
options: Surface
700 ft AGL
1,200 ft AGL
10,000 ft MSL
questions.explanation: 700 ft AGL in the shaded magenta. Below 700 is Class G (surface to 699). Outside the shaded magenta, Class E by default below 18,000 MSL.

questions.question: 5 SM visibility AND 1 SM horizontal cloud clearance above 10,000 — don't those cancel?
options: Yes — only the more restrictive number applies
No — 5 SM is your overall visibility minimum; 1,000/1,000/1 SM is your separation from clouds. At altitude there are no stacked cloud rollers, so clearance numbers grow
No — you only need to meet one of them
Yes — the FAA accidentally over-specified this rule
questions.explanation: Different requirements stacking. 5 SM = overall visibility minimum. 1,000/1,000/1 SM = separation FROM clouds. At altitude there are no stacked cloud layers, so the clearance numbers grow to give pilots maneuvering room.

questions.question: DPE scenario: 'You're at a Class G airport getting dinner. Metar reports 2 SM visibility, broken at 2,000 ft. It's now nighttime. Can you depart?'
options: Yes — 1 SM clear of clouds is enough
No — at night Class G needs 3 SM + 500 below / 1,000 above / 2,000 horizontal. With 2 SM viz and a 2,000-ft layer you can't meet the visibility minimum
Yes — visibility is the only requirement
Yes — but only if you stay below 1,200 AGL
questions.explanation: At night Class G requires 3 SM + 500 below / 1,000 above / 2,000 horizontal — significantly more than day's 1 SM clear of clouds. With reported 2 SM you're trapped at the field until conditions improve. Pattern: DPE tests situational awareness, you read the chart in context.
